one_line_takeaway: Digger detects copyrighted-book membership in an LLM's training data via a reference-LLM 'loss gap' calibrated with Wasserstein distance, reaching AUC 0.912 and ~84.75% accuracy on GPT2-XL in controlled tests, but is validated only on small/medium models with no ground-truth labels and no comparison to prior membership-inference baselines.
key_finding: In the controlled mixed-membership experiment on GPT2-XL, Digger separates seen from unseen target samples with an AUC of 0.912 (the paper's key-findings box states 0.914 - a minor internal inconsistency). Sweeping the false-positive rate, at FPR=20% (threshold 0.128) it reaches accuracy 84.750%, TPR 89.500% and F1 85.442%, and at FPR=25% (threshold 0.103) TPR rises to 92.428% (these two rows are the source of the 'accuracy 84.750% / recall 92.428%' figures quoted in the intro). For purely-unseen target data, 83.33% (2,499/3,000) of samples land in the lowest confidence bin (0-0.1); for purely-seen data, 86.67% (2,600/3,000) land in the highest bin (0.9-1.0). Underlying detectability scales strongly with model size, token length and repetition: AUC ranges from 0.67318 (GPT-2 Small, 1 repeat, 50 tokens) to 0.99995 (GPT2-XL, 3 repeats, 100 tokens). In the real-world test, Digger judges famous quotes/poetry as likely present in both GPT2-XL and LLaMA-7b (e.g., 76/500 GPT2-XL and 84/500 LLaMA-7b samples fall in the 0.9-1.0 confidence bin).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Digger, a framework that detects whether content from potentially copyrighted books was present in an LLM's training set and outputs a per-sample confidence score for its inclusion. The technical novelty is the 'loss gap' / reference-LLM calibration procedure: rather than thresholding raw sample loss on the target model (which the authors, following Mireshghallah et al. [15], argue is unreliable), Digger constructs a baseline LLM, a reference LLM, a reference-tuned LLM and a vanilla-tuned LLM, then uses the Wasserstein distance between the reference-tuned and vanilla-tuned loss distributions to calibrate a standard reference distribution from which a confidence score and an FPR-controlled threshold are derived. This is a specialized membership-inference / data-provenance auditing method for the book-copyright setting. The framing on prior book-copyright work (Chang et al. [5], 'Speak, Memory', the only cited prior study on book copyright in LLMs) and on memorization work (Carlini et al. [3,4]) is Related Work, not this paper's contribution; Digger's own novelty is the reference-LLM loss-gap calibration and confidence estimation.
threat_model: Auditing / detection setting rather than an attacker capability. The concern is an LLM trainer who, intentionally or not, includes copyrighted book text in an undisclosed pre-training or fine-tuning corpus. The auditor (e.g., a copyright holder or third party) has black-box-plus access sufficient to fine-tune the target 'vanilla' LLM and to compute per-sample language-modeling (cross-entropy) loss, but does NOT have ground-truth labels for what was actually in the original training set. The goal is to infer membership of specific copyrighted passages in the model's prior training data. This is a membership-inference-style privacy/IP auditing threat model, not an adversarial evasion or poisoning model.
limitations: (1) No ground-truth labels for the real GPT-2/LLaMA training corpora, so real-world 'likely trained on' claims cannot be directly verified (the authors' own Internal Threat). (2) Only small-to-medium models evaluated (GPT-2 up to 1.5B, LLaMA-7b); larger models (GPT-3, LLaMA-13B/30B) untested due to compute cost. (3) High computational and storage cost: the method requires selecting many unlearned samples and fine-tuning the target LLM multiple times plus repeated inference. (4) Confidence estimation assumes the unlearned-dataset-I loss-difference distribution is normal; alternatives (kernel density estimation, Bayesian inference, bootstrapping) are only noted as future work. (5) No comparison against existing membership-inference baselines - the term 'baseline' in the paper refers to Digger's own internal baseline LLM/distribution, not a competing method, so there is no head-to-head efficacy benchmark. (6) Copyright legality is jurisdiction-dependent; the authors treat this as orthogonal to the detection technique. (7) Minor reporting inconsistency between the 0.912 AUC in text and 0.914 in the key-findings box.

datasets_used: 5 novels published in 2023 (characteristic study; Appendix A: Happy Place, Clytemnestra, Godkiller #1, Said No One Ever, Some Desperate Glory) -> 100 unique 500-word passages
120 post-2023 novels from Z-Library, split into Baseline (35), Unlearned I (35), Target (3 versions of 15 novels each), and Unlearned II

500 real-world excerpts as the single real-world target dataset, scored separately against GPT2-XL and LLaMA-7b (paper is inconsistent on the source: SS5.1.1 calls them '500 excerpts sourced from Goodreads', SS5.2/RQ4 calls them '500 segments of poetry... along with excerpts from... literary masterpieces' -- one 500-item set, not two)
WebText (the corpus GPT-2 was originally pre-trained on; ~40GB English web text)
quantitative_results: Controlled mixed-membership AUC on GPT2-XL: 0.912 (text) / 0.914 (key-findings box)
GPT2-XL at FPR=20% (threshold 0.128): accuracy 84.750%, TPR 89.500%, FNR 10.500%, F1 85.442%
GPT2-XL at FPR=25% (threshold 0.103): TPR 92.428%, FNR 7.571%, F1 85.048%
GPT2-XL at FPR=5% (threshold 0.460): accuracy 69.857%, TPR 44.714%, F1 59.733%
Confidence binning (3,000 samples each): purely-unseen 83.33% (2,499) in (0,0.1); purely-seen 86.67% (2,600) in (0.9,1.0)
AUC table range: min 0.67318 (GPT-2 Small, 1 repeat, 50 tokens); max 0.99995 (GPT2-XL, 3 repeats, 100 tokens)
Real-world high-confidence (0.9-1.0) counts out of 500: GPT2-XL 76, LLaMA-7b 84
Implementation: ~3,000 lines of PyTorch, single 40GB A100
baselines_compared: No external/competing membership-inference method is used as a baseline (the paper's 'baseline LLM'/'baseline dataset' are internal reference components, not comparison methods)
Internal ablation-style comparisons across GPT-2 sizes (Small/Medium/Large/XL) and LLaMA-7b, across token lengths 50-100 and repetition counts 1/2/3
Implicit conceptual contrast with raw sample-loss thresholding (argued unreliable per Mireshghallah et al. [15]), which motivates the reference-LLM calibration
